source_document: ASML acquisition filing Nov 2013 / ASML press releases
derivation_method: ASML acquired Cymer in Nov 2013 for ~$2.5B cash; Cymer's annual DUV excimer-laser light-source revenue estimated at ~$2.6B standalone. Shown as sub-component supplier reflecting embedded cost centre within ASML's current manufacturing.
citation_excerpt: ASML 2013 press: 'ASML completes acquisition of Cymer for ~$2.5B'. Cymer was sole supplier of DUV ArF/KrF excimer laser sources to ASML prior to full consolidation.
data_caveat: Cymer is now a 100%-owned ASML subsidiary (since Nov 2013). This flow represents the historical/embedded commercial value of the light-source sub-system, not an arm's-length transaction. Shown to match YouTube creator's taxonomy.

source_document: Pfeiffer Vacuum (Atlas Copco sub.) / industry estimates

data_caveat: Pfeiffer acquired by Atlas Copco 2021; no longer independently listed. Semi revenue not separately disclosed.

derivation_method: ~25% of Pfeiffer semi revenue ($462M) allocated to AMAT CVD/etch/PVD tools.

derivation_method: Intel Foundry segment ~$17.5B FY2025 (almost entirely intersegment). Represents manufacturing services value transferred from Intel Fabs to CCG + DCAI product divisions.

data_caveat: Intel Foundry is 99%+ internal — Intel uses its own fabs to make Core and Xeon chips. Operating loss ~$9B annual run-rate in FY2025. 'Intel remains the only major customer of its own 18A node.'

derivation_method: External Intel Foundry revenue annualised: $85M first nine months FY2025 → ~$113M; FY2024 $307M (incl. $222M product revenue). Used $0.30B as representative estimate.
citation_excerpt: Intel 10-K Q3 FY2025: 'External foundry revenue $32M in Q3 2025; $85M in first nine months.' FY2024 external was $307M incl. $222M from Intel's own IP licensing as a product.
data_caveat: Intel external foundry customers not named publicly. Intel 18A yield ramp is critical to attracting meaningful external customers beyond the current pilot phase.

source_document: TSMC 2025 20-F; Intel product disclosures; industry analyst reports
derivation_method: Intel outsources certain products to TSMC: Arc GPU (N6/N5 node), select connectivity and networking chips. Estimated ~2.8% of TSMC FY2024 revenue = $2.5B. Extracted from TSMC Other Top-10 ($16.28B → $13.78B, −$2.50B).
citation_excerpt: Intel Arc GPU (Alchemist, Battlemage) confirmed manufactured at TSMC N6/N5. TSMC 20-F historically showed Intel in top-3 customers (~5-7%) in 2022–2023; share declined as Intel Foundry First strategy evolved.
data_caveat: Intel TSMC spend declining from earlier peak as more Intel designs move to TSMC N3/N2 for outsourced products while Intel internal 18A/20A ramps for Core/Xeon.

source_document: Samsung Foundry capacity estimates; Qualcomm chipset sourcing reports
derivation_method: Samsung Foundry total ~$17B analyst est.; Qualcomm ~$2B (legacy Snapdragon at Samsung S4/S5 Austin TX and Hwaseong Korea fabs at 4nm node).
citation_excerpt: Qualcomm shifted majority of Snapdragon 8-series to TSMC N3/N4 from ~2021; Samsung retained mid-range Snapdragon designs and some legacy production.
data_caveat: Samsung Foundry revenue is NOT separately disclosed (only consolidated DS Division $92.9B). $17B total is analyst estimate from Gartner and IDC foundry market share data.

source_document: Samsung Foundry capacity estimates; Samsung DS Division FY2025 disclosure
derivation_method: Samsung Foundry ~$17B est. − $2B Qualcomm = $15B. Customers: own Exynos AP (Samsung Mobile, largest internal customer), Tesla (Autopilot SoC), IBM, NXP.
citation_excerpt: Samsung DS Division FY2025: total $92.9B; Memory $74.4B; implied Foundry ~$17B. Samsung Foundry market share ~12-15% globally (vs TSMC ~60%).
data_caveat: Samsung Foundry market share eroded in 2024-25 due to yield and HBM qualification issues. Internal Exynos is by far the largest single Samsung Foundry customer. Tesla Autopilot SoC (FSD chip) is manufactured at Samsung Foundry.
